text: The surgeon utilizes a robotic system with a forearm approach to access the surgical site for hernia repair.
The surgeon begins the hernia repair procedure by making an incision to open the peritoneum at the Douglas pouch, exposing the underlying structures.
The surgeon dissects the rectovaginal space and the anterior part of the rectum, carefully separating these anatomical structures during the hernia repair procedure.
The surgeon performs a lateral dissection on the left side of the rectum, revealing both sides of the muscle defect in the pelvic floor.
The global view reveals the fully dissected muscle defect in the pelvic floor, with clear visualization of the surrounding anatomical structures.
The surgeon performs a peritoneal incision at the level of the sacral promontory, using robotic instruments positioned as previously described.
The surgeon dissects the retrorectal space, exposing the pelvic floor and revealing the anatomical structures within this area.
The surgeon joins the dissections of the rectovaginal space and the lateral dissection on the left side of the rectum, unifying the surgical field around the muscle defect of the pelvic floor.
The surgeon performs a digital rectal examination, visually revealing the perineal hernia followed by the rectum.

The surgeon places a biological mesh over the dissected muscle defect, securing it by suturing the proximal end to the sacral promontory in a posterior rectopexy fashion, effectively pulling the pelvic floor into position.
The surgeon uses interrupted Vicryl IIO sutures to secure the biological mesh to the lateral rectal wall and the pelvic floor, ensuring stable fixation of the mesh within the surgical field.
The surgeon secures the proximal end of the biological mesh to the promontory using two Vicryl IIO sutures, completing the fixation in a posterior rectopexy fashion.
The surgeon closes the peritoneum using two running barbed sutures of PDS-III-O, completing the final step of the procedure.
